justification: All three ACs present with exact values ($47.80 -> 47, $0.99 -> 0, 12-month expiry). Both domain rules present and precise. Both architecture functions referenced (earnPoints, checkExpiry) with implementation notes including Math.floor and loyalty.ts file location. Terminology alignment flags the 365-day vs 12-month discrepancy and notes leap-year consideration. No invented information — all content traces back to plausible Bird/MJ outputs.

observations.text: All three acceptance criteria preserved with exact dollar values and expected point outcomes
Both domain rules clearly stated with testable assertions that embed the AC values
Architecture guidance includes earnPoints/checkExpiry signatures, Math.floor usage, loyalty.ts location, and no-dependency constraint
Terminology alignment flags 365-day vs 12-month difference and explicitly mentions leap-year edge case
Open questions are well-scoped and actionable — boundary condition, batch vs single-customer, and data contract

Terminology alignment slightly softens the tension by saying they are 'aligned for implementation purposes' rather than explicitly flagging them as potentially non-equivalent — but does mention leap-year caveat, so this is minor
The 'points marked expired not deleted' observation and suggested status flag pattern is a valuable synthesis detail traced to Bird's wording

handoff_brief:
  recipient: Shaq
  task_context: >
    Implement a loyalty points module in TypeScript. Customers earn points on
    purchases (1 point per dollar, floor-rounded) and lose all points if their
    account goes inactive for 12 months. The module is self-contained with no
    external dependencies — two pure functions in a single file.

  domain_rules:
    - rule: A customer earns 1 point per dollar spent, rounded down to the nearest integer.
      testable_assertion: earnPo
